## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a vascular graft. List four essential design criteria that a vascular prosthesis must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**
A vascular graft is a tubular prosthetic device used to replace, bypass, or repair a diseased or damaged segment of an artery or vein, restoring physiological blood flow.

Four essential design criteria:
1. **Biocompatibility** — non-thrombogenic luminal surface; no chronic immune response.
2. **Mechanical compliance match** with the host vessel to minimise anastomotic intimal hyperplasia.
3. **Adequate burst strength** (sustains > 4,000 mmHg) and **suture retention strength** (> 2 N).
4. **Sterilisability and shelf-life stability** without loss of mechanical or chemical properties.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between pyrolytic carbon and graphite. Why is pyrolytic carbon the preferred material for mechanical heart valve occluders?**

**Model Answer:**
Graphite is a crystalline form of carbon with a layered hexagonal lattice and weak interlayer van der Waals bonds. Pyrolytic carbon is an **isotropic, turbostratic** form deposited by chemical vapour deposition of hydrocarbons at ~1,500 °C — it lacks the long-range order of graphite.

Pyrolytic carbon is preferred because it offers:
- High **thromboresistance** (non-thrombogenic luminal surface).
- Exceptional **wear and fatigue resistance** under 2.5 billion loading cycles.
- **Isotropic mechanical properties** (uniform strength in all directions).
- Excellent **biocompatibility and chemical inertness** in physiological environments.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Discuss the materials used for vascular grafts. Compare the performance of Dacron (PET) and ePTFE grafts with respect to porosity, healing characteristics, and clinical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is constructed using an autologous saphenous vein segment of internal diameter 3 mm and length 12 cm. Given a mean pressure drop of 35 mmHg, blood viscosity $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and density $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, calculate the volumetric flow rate, mean velocity, and Reynolds number. Comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Materials for vascular grafts** *(7 marks)*

| Material | Structure | Porosity | Healing | Indication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dacron (PET)** | Woven or knitted | High (knitted) → requires pre-clotting; low (woven) | Promotes tissue ingrowth through pores | Aorta, iliac (large diameter, > 8 mm) |
| **ePTFE (Gore-Tex®)** | Expanded microporous node-fibril | Internodal distance ~ 30 µm; effectively non-porous to blood | Limited tissue ingrowth; forms thin fibrin pseudointima | Above-knee femoro-popliteal bypass, dialysis AV grafts |
| **Autograft (saphenous vein / IMA)** | Native tissue | Zero (no leakage) | Endothelialised, near-zero thrombogenicity | CABG; small-diameter positions |
| **Biological xenograft (bovine carotid / HUV)** | Crosslinked collagen | Pre-formed matrix | Slow remodelling, prone to aneurysmal degeneration | Salvage cases when autograft unavailable |

- **Dacron** is preferred in large-diameter, high-flow, low-resistance positions (aorta) because of its superior burst strength and easy suturing to thick arterial walls. It requires **pre-clotting** (passing the patient's own blood through the graft before implantation) to seal the knitted interstices.
- **ePTFE** is preferred in medium-diameter grafts (6 – 8 mm) where its microporous structure yields a smoother, less permeable luminal surface and better handling characteristics.
- Both fail in **< 6 mm diameter** due to compliance mismatch and thrombogenesis — the saphenous vein autograft remains the only reliable option here.

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

Given: $D = 3$ mm → $R = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 0.12$ m, $\Delta P = 35 \times 133.32 = 4666.2$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Flow rate (Hagen–Poiseuille):**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 4666.2 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}{8 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12}$$

Numerator: $\pi \times 4666.2 \times 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} = 7.42 \times 10^{-8}$  
Denominator: $3.36 \times 10^{-3}$

$$Q = \frac{7.42 \times 10^{-8}}{3.36 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.21 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 1.33 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 2 — Mean velocity:**

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{2.21 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} = \frac{2.21 \times 10^{-5}}{7.07 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3.12 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu} = \frac{1060 \times 3.12 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{9.92}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2834$$

**Step 4 — Flow regime comment:**

Since $Re \approx 2834 > 2000$, the flow regime is **turbulent (or transitional)**. This is clinically undesirable — a CABG ideally operates in laminar flow ($Re < 2000$). The high mean velocity and turbulent regime suggest the chosen graft diameter is borderline; a slightly larger conduit or reduced flow demand would improve haemodynamic compatibility.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Classify prosthetic heart valves. Compare mechanical and bioprosthetic valves in terms of material, durability, thrombogenicity, and indications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient with severe aortic stenosis has a measured transvalvular mean pressure gradient of 40 mmHg and a root-mean-square flow rate of 250 mL/s. Using the **Gorlin equation** for the aortic position, calculate the effective orifice area (EOA) and classify the severity of stenosis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Classification of prosthetic heart valves** *(7 marks)*

The classification tree is:
- **Mechanical valves**
  - *Ball-and-cage* (e.g., Starr–Edwards, 1960s)
  - *Tilting-disc* (e.g., Björk–Shiley, Medtronic Hall)
  - *Bileaflet* (e.g., St. Jude Medical, ATS, On-X) — **most commonly implanted today**
- **Bioprosthetic (tissue) valves**
  - *Stented porcine* (e.g., Hancock, Carpentier–Edwards)
  - *Stentless porcine* (e.g., Medtronic Freestyle)
  - *Bovine pericardial* (e.g., Carpentier–Edwards Perimount)
  - *Homograft (allograft)*
  - *Pulmonary autograft* (Ross procedure)

| Parameter | Mechanical | Bioprosthetic |
|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Pyrolytic carbon occluder + Ti housing + Dacron sewing cuff | Glutaraldehyde-crosslinked porcine/bovine tissue + polymer/metal stent |
| **Durability** | ≥ 30 years | 10 – 15 years (structural valve deterioration) |
| **Thrombogenicity** | **High** → lifelong warfarin (INR 2.5 – 3.5) | Low → 3 months anticoagulation only |
| **Indication** | Patients < 60 years, no anticoagulation contraindication | Patients > 65 years, women of child-bearing age, anticoagulation contraindications |

**(b) Gorlin equation calculation** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- $\Delta P_{mean} = 40$ mmHg
- $Q_{rms} = 250$ mL/s

**Step 2 — Apply the aortic Gorlin equation:**

$$\text{EOA}_{aortic} = \frac{Q_{rms}}{51.6 \sqrt{\Delta P_{mean}}}$$

$$\text{EOA} = \frac{250}{51.6 \times \sqrt{40}} = \frac{250}{51.6 \times 6.3246} = \frac{250}{326.3} = 0.766 \text{ cm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Severity classification (clinical thresholds):**

| EOA Range | Severity |
|---|---|
| > 1.5 cm² | Mild stenosis |
| 1.0 – 1.5 cm² | Moderate stenosis |
| 0.8 – 1.0 cm² | Severe stenosis |
| < 0.8 cm² | **Critical stenosis** |

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

Since EOA ≈ **0.77 cm² < 0.8 cm²**, the patient has **critical aortic stenosis** with severe prosthesis-patient mismatch. **Clinical recommendation:** valve replacement surgery (SAVR) or transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) is indicated to relieve the obstruction and prevent sudden cardiac death.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students frequently use the constant **44.3** (mitral value) instead of **51.6** (aortic value) in the Gorlin equation. Always check the **valve position** before substituting. Marks are deducted if the wrong constant is used without justification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** A common error is converting $\Delta P$ from mmHg to Pa before substituting into the Gorlin equation. The Gorlin equation is **empirically calibrated for mmHg** — **do NOT convert**. Marks are lost for unnecessary unit conversion in this context.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** In vascular graft questions, students often forget to specify the **no-slip boundary condition** when deriving Hagen–Poiseuille. This is a non-negotiable assumption and earns full marks only when explicitly stated.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Vascular grafts** are tubular conduits; **stents** are intraluminal scaffolds; **stent-grafts** are hybrid devices for EVAR.
- ☐ The **6 mm rule**: synthetic grafts show acceptable patency only for diameters **> 6 mm**; below this, use **autograft** (saphenous vein / IMA).
- ☐ **Dacron (PET)** = high burst strength, used in aorta; **ePTFE** = microporous, used in femoro-popliteal positions.
- ☐ **Compliance mismatch** between graft and native vessel is the leading cause of **anastomotic intimal hyperplasia**.
- ☐ **Hagen–Poiseuille:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L}$ — flow scales with the **fourth power** of radius.
- ☐ **Wall shear stress (WSS):** Physiological range **1.5 – 2.0 Pa**; outside this range → endothelial dysfunction or platelet activation.
- ☐ **Reynolds number:** $Re < 2{,}000$ laminar; $Re > 2{,}000$ turbulent. KTU often asks for **flow regime classification**.
- ☐ **Heart valve classes:** Mechanical (durable, thrombogenic) vs. Bioprosthetic (less durable, non-thrombogenic).
- ☐ **Pyrolytic carbon** is the gold-standard mechanical valve occluder material — isotropic, thromboresistant, fatigue-resistant.
- ☐ **Bileaflet mechanical valves** (e.g., St. Jude) are the most commonly implanted mechanical valves today.
- ☐ **Glutaraldehyde crosslinking** of bioprosthetic tissue reduces antigenicity but promotes **calcification** over time.
- ☐ **Gorlin equation (aortic):** $\text{EOA} = \dfrac{Q_{rms}}{51.6 \sqrt{\Delta P_{mean}}}$ — EOA < 0.8 cm² = **critical stenosis**.
- ☐ **Anticoagulation rule:** Mechanical valve → lifelong warfarin (INR 2.5 – 3.5); Bioprosthetic valve → 3 months only.
- ☐ **Ross procedure** is preferred for children/young adults — uses the patient's own pulmonary valve to replace the aortic valve.
- ☐ **Prosthesis–Patient Mismatch (PPM):** indexed EOA < 0.65 cm²/m² → increased mortality; avoid undersizing the prosthesis.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT412 - BIOMATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swelling and leaching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Swelling in Biomaterials

### Formal Definition
**Swelling** is the thermodynamic process by which a polymeric biomaterial (most commonly a hydrogel, scaffold, or crosslinked network) absorbs and retains a significant volume of water or physiological fluid, leading to a measurable increase in mass, volume, and dimensional footprint, while preserving its structural coherence due to the presence of physical or chemical crosslinks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Exact):** Swelling is defined as the uptake of physiological fluid by a hydrophilic polymer network, governed by the balance between the **osmotic driving force** (favoring solvent ingress) and the **elastic retractile response of the crosslinked network** (opposing it). The Flory–Rehner theory quantitatively relates the equilibrium degree of swelling to the crosslink density and polymer–solvent interaction parameter.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Imagine a **kitchen sponge made of jelly** placed in a bowl of water. The water rushes in and is held captive inside the jelly matrix. The jelly swells, becomes soft and pliable, but does not dissolve because the crosslinks act like tiny internal "anchors" holding the whole structure together. In biomaterials, the "jelly" is a hydrophilic polymer such as poly(HEMA) used in contact lenses, PVA in wound dressings, or alginate in tissue scaffolds. The "anchors" are the chemical crosslinks (covalent bonds) or physical entanglements (crystalline domains, ionic bridges).

## 1.2 Leaching in Biomaterials

### Formal Definition
**Leaching** is the mass-transfer process by which soluble constituents (unreacted monomers, residual catalysts, oligomers, plasticizers, degradation byproducts, or encapsulated drug molecules) diffuse out of a bulk biomaterial into the surrounding physiological medium, driven by a **concentration gradient**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Exact):** Leaching is the diffusion-controlled release of low-molecular-weight species (additives, initiators, unreacted monomers, fillers, drug payload, or degradation fragments) from a biomatrix into the external bioenvironment. It is critical to both **biocompatibility assessment** (toxic leachables) and **controlled drug delivery** (therapeutic release).

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Think of a **tea bag dipped in hot water**. The colored tannins and flavor molecules diffuse out of the bag into the surrounding water. Similarly, a biomaterial implant (e.g., a PMMA bone cement) submerged in body fluid will release trace amounts of residual methyl methacrylate monomer, benzoyl peroxide initiator, or stabilizer. In drug delivery, this same principle is exploited in a *positive* sense—a loaded drug payload deliberately leaches out of a polymer matrix to treat a disease.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Misconception (KTU pitfall):** Students often confuse leaching with degradation. Leaching involves only **physical diffusion** of an existing soluble species; the polymer backbone itself remains intact. Degradation involves **chemical bond cleavage** of the polymer chain. The two may occur simultaneously (e.g., a degrading PLA scaffold also leaches lactic acid oligomers).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bold for emphasis)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Equilibrium mass swelling ratio | $q_m$ | dimensionless |
| Equilibrium volume swelling ratio | $q_v$ | dimensionless |
| Crosslink density | $\rho_x$ | $\mathbf{mol \cdot m^{-3}}$ |
| Mesh size of network | $\xi$ | $\mathbf{nm}$ |
| Diffusion coefficient of penetrant | $D$ | $\mathbf{m^{2} \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| Flory–Huggins interaction parameter | $\chi$ | dimensionless |
| Leaching flux | $J$ | $\mathbf{mol \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| Partition coefficient | $K$ | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Swelling: The Three Competing Forces

The swelling behavior of a crosslinked polymer network in a thermodynamically compatible solvent is governed by the balance of **three free-energy contributions**, formalized by Flory and Rehner (1943):

1. **Mixing Free Energy ($\Delta G_{mix}$)** — the osmotic driving force that pulls solvent into the network. Predicted by the **Flory–Huggins lattice theory**.

2. **Elastic Free Energy ($\Delta G_{el}$)** — the retractive force exerted by the stretched crosslinked chains opposing further expansion.

3. **Ionic (Polyelectrolyte) Free Energy ($\Delta G_{ion}$)** — additional osmotic contribution from counter-ions in charged (anionic/cationic) hydrogels (e.g., sodium alginate, PAA).

> **Equilibrium condition:** At swelling equilibrium, the chemical potential of solvent inside the network equals that of the external bath.
>
> $$\Delta \mu_{swollen}^{network} = \Delta \mu_{external}^{bath} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial (\Delta G_{total})}{\partial n_1} = 0$$

The total free energy of the gel is:

$$\Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_{mix} + \Delta G_{el} + \Delta G_{ion}$$

## 2.2 Mass and Volume Swelling Ratios

**Mass swelling ratio** (experimentally measured by gravimetry):

$$q_m = \frac{m_s - m_d}{m_d} = \frac{m_{solvent}}{m_{polymer}}$$

**Volume swelling ratio** (related to mass ratio through densities $\rho_p$ and $\rho_s$):

$$q_v = 1 + \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_s}(q_m - 1)$$

**Degree of swelling** (mass of solvent per unit mass of swollen gel):

$$\text{DS} = \frac{m_s - m_d}{m_s} = \frac{q_m}{1 + q_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** If the swollen mass is 5× the dry mass, then $q_m = 4$ and DS = 0.8 (i.e., the gel is 80% water by total mass).

## 2.3 Flory–Rehner Equation (Most Critical KTU Formula)

For a **neutral, crosslinked polymer** swollen in a good solvent at equilibrium:

$$\boxed{\;-\ln(1 - v_{2,s}) + v_{2,s} + \chi v_{2,s}^{\,2} = V_1 \, n \, \left[ v_{2,s}^{\,1/3} - \frac{v_{2,s}}{2} \right]\;}$$

where:
- $v_{2,s}$ = volume fraction of **polymer in the swollen gel at equilibrium** (note: $v_{2,s} = 1/q_v$)
- $\chi$ = Flory–Huggins polymer–solvent interaction parameter
- $V_1$ = molar volume of the solvent (water $\approx 18 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathbf{m^{3} \cdot mol^{-1}}$)
- $n$ = effective number of chains per unit volume (proportional to crosslink density)

**Inverse form** (used to compute crosslink density from a measured $q_v$):

$$n = \frac{-\ln(1 - v_{2,s}) + v_{2,s} + \chi v_{2,s}^{\,2}}{V_1 \left( v_{2,s}^{\,1/3} - v_{2,s}/2 \right)}$$

The **average molecular weight between crosslinks** $M_c$ is related by:

$$M_c = \frac{\rho_p}{n}$$

## 2.4 Mesh Size of the Network

The **mesh (or pore) size** $\xi$ controls solute diffusion, drug release, and cell infiltration:

$$\xi = v_{2,s}^{\,-1/3} \cdot \ell \left( \frac{2 C_n M_c}{M_0} \right)^{1/2}$$

where $\ell \approx 0.154\ \mathbf{nm}$ is the C–C bond length, $C_n$ is the Flory characteristic ratio, and $M_0$ is the molecular weight of the polymer repeat unit.

## 2.5 Swelling Kinetics — Diffusion Mechanisms

The time-dependent swelling of a thin slab/cylinder/sphere is described by a **power-law** (Peppas–Sahlin model):

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = k \, t^n$$

| Diffusion exponent $n$ (thin slab) | Mechanism | KTU label |
|---|---|---|
| $n = 0.5$ | **Fickian (Case I)** diffusion | Diffusion-controlled |
| $0.5 < n < 1.0$ | **Anomalous (Non-Fickian)** | Both diffusion & relaxation |
| $n = 1.0$ | **Case II** transport | Relaxation-controlled |
| $n > 1.0$ | **Super Case II** | Stress-cracking dominant |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For a **cylindrical** sample, Fickian gives $n = 0.45$; for a **spherical** sample, Fickian gives $n = 0.43$. Remember to specify geometry!

## 2.6 Leaching: Fickian Diffusion Framework

For a **planar biomaterial slab** (thickness $2L$) initially loaded uniformly with a leachable species of concentration $C_0$, immersed in a large bath (sink condition, $C_{ext} \approx 0$), the **cumulative fraction released** at time $t$ is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^{2}} \exp\!\left[ -D (2n+1)^{2} \pi^{2} t \,/\, 4L^{2} \right]\;}$$

**Short-time approximation** (Fickian release, valid for $M_t/M_\infty \leq 0.6$):

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{2}{L} \sqrt{\frac{D\,t}{\pi}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{D\,t}{\pi L^{2}}}$$

**Steady-state flux** (Fick's first law):

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \frac{D \, K \, C_0}{L}$$

where $K$ is the **partition coefficient** of the leachable between the polymer and the external medium.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_m = (m_s - m_d)/m_d$ | dimensionless | Mass swelling ratio |
| 2 | $q_v = 1 + (\rho_p/\rho_s)(q_m - 1)$ | dimensionless | Volume swelling ratio |
| 3 | $\Delta G = \Delta G_{mix} + \Delta G_{el} + \Delta G_{ion}$ | J | Free energy balance |
| 4 | Flory–Rehner (neutral) | $v_{2,s}, \chi, V_1, n$ | Crosslink density from $q_v$ |
| 5 | $n = \rho_p / M_c$ | $\mathbf{mol/m^{3}}$ | Network chain density |
| 6 | $\xi = v_{2,s}^{-1/3} \cdot \ell (2C_n M_c / M_0)^{1/2}$ | $\mathbf{nm}$ | Mesh size |
| 7 | $M_t/M_\infty = k t^n$ | dimensionless | Swelling/release kinetics |
| 8 | $J = D K C_0 / L$ | $\mathbf{mol\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}}$ | Leaching flux (steady state) |
| 9 | $D_{eff} = D_0 \exp(-\beta v_{2,s})$ | $\mathbf{m^{2}/s}$ | Diffusion in swollen gel |
| 10 | $\tau = L^{2}/D$ | $\mathbf{s}$ | Characteristic diffusion time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Swelling and leaching dictate the **performance envelope** of virtually every hydrogel-based device on the market—soft contact lenses (oxygen permeability vs. water content), transdermal patches (drug leaching flux), nerve guidance channels (scaffold swelling to fill defect), and bone cements (toxic monomer leaching, < 2% residual MMA mandated by ISO 5833).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Flory–Rehner Equilibrium Swelling Equation

We start with the **Flory–Huggins mixing free energy** per unit volume for polymer + solvent:

$$\Delta G_{mix} = k_B T \left[ n_1 \ln v_1 + n_2 \ln v_2 + \chi \, n_1 v_2 \right]$$

where $n_1$ is solvent moles, $n_2$ is polymer moles, $v_1$ and $v_2$ are volume fractions, and $\chi$ is the interaction parameter.

**Step 1:** Express $\Delta G_{mix}$ per unit volume of polymer network in terms of $v_2$ (polymer volume fraction) and the molar volume of solvent $V_1$:

$$\frac{\Delta G_{mix}}{V} = \frac{RT}{V_1} \left[ (1 - v_2)\ln(1 - v_2) + \chi \, v_2 (1 - v_2) \right]$$

**Step 2:** The **elastic free energy** of a phantom network (Flory theory) on swelling from a dry state to a swollen state with isotropic expansion factor $\alpha$:

$$\Delta G_{el} = \frac{3}{2} n k_B T \left( \alpha^{2} - 1 \right) - n k_B T \ln \alpha^{3}$$

For a dry network (volume $V_0$) swelling to volume $V_s$, the expansion factor along each axis is:

$$\alpha = \left( \frac{V_s}{V_0} \right)^{1/3} = v_{2,s}^{\,-1/3}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\alpha = v_{2,s}^{\,-1/3}$ and simplify (ignoring the $\ln \alpha^{3}$ term for low crosslink density):

$$\Delta G_{el} = \frac{3}{2} n k_B T \left( v_{2,s}^{\,-2/3} - 1 \right) \approx \frac{3}{2} n k_B T \left( v_{2,s}^{\,-2/3} - 1 \right)$$

Per unit volume of the network:

$$\frac{\Delta G_{el}}{V_0} = \frac{3}{2} n k_B T \, v_{2,s} \left( v_{2,s}^{\,-2/3} - 1 \right) = \frac{3}{2} n k_B T \left( v_{2,s}^{\,1/3} - v_{2,s} \right)$$

**Step 4:** At swelling equilibrium for a neutral network ($\Delta G_{ion} = 0$), set $\partial \Delta G_{total}/\partial v_2 = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial v_2}\!\left[ \frac{\Delta G_{mix}}{V} + \frac{\Delta G_{el}}{V_0} \right] = 0$$

Computing the derivative term by term:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial v_2}\!\left[ \frac{RT}{V_1}(1 - v_2)\ln(1 - v_2) + \chi v_2(1 - v_2) \right] = \frac{RT}{V_1}\!\left[ -\ln(1 - v_2) - 1 + \chi(1 - 2v_2) \right]$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial v_2}\!\left[ \frac{3}{2} n k_B T \left( v_{2,s}^{\,1/3} - v_{2,s} \right) \right] = \frac{3}{2} n k_B T \left( \tfrac{1}{3} v_{2,s}^{\,-2/3} - 1 \right) = \frac{n k_B T}{2}\!\left( v_{2,s}^{\,-2/3} - 3 \right)$$

**Step 5:** Set the sum to zero. Convert $k_B$ to $R$ via $R = N_A k_B$, and note that $V_1 N_A = $ molar volume, and $n N_A = $ moles per unit volume. After standard algebraic simplification (multiply through by $V_1/(RT)$):

$$\boxed{\;-\ln(1 - v_{2,s}) + v_{2,s} + \chi \, v_{2,s}^{\,2} = V_1 \, n \left( v_{2,s}^{\,1/3} - \tfrac{1}{2} v_{2,s} \right)\;}$$

> This is the **Flory–Rehner equation** quoted in KTU examinations. The LHS is the **mixing (osmotic) contribution**; the RHS is the **elastic (retractive) contribution**.

## 3.2 Derivation B: Short-Time Fickian Leaching from a Slab

We solve **Fick's second law** in one dimension for a planar slab of half-thickness $L$ (initial uniform loading $C_0$, both faces exposed to a perfect sink):

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}, \quad -L \leq x \leq L$$

**Boundary conditions:**

$$C(\pm L,\, t) = 0 \quad (\text{sink}), \qquad C(x,\, 0) = C_0 \quad (\text{uniform initial loading})$$

**Step 1:** Separate variables: $C(x,t) = X(x)\,T(t)$. Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{D\,T}\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{1}{X}\frac{d^{2} X}{dx^{2}} = -\lambda^{2}$$

This gives two ODEs:
- $dT/dt = -D \lambda^{2} T \Rightarrow T(t) = A\,e^{-D \lambda^{2} t}$
- $d^{2}X/dx^{2} = -\lambda^{2} X \Rightarrow X(x) = B\cos(\lambda x) + E\sin(\lambda x)$

**Step 2:** Apply sink BC $X(\pm L) = 0$. The cosine term gives $B \cos(\lambda L) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_n = (2n+1)\pi/(2L)$. The sine term is odd, so $E = 0$.

$$X_n(x) = B_n \cos\!\left[ \frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Superpose all eigenmodes and satisfy the initial condition $C(x,0) = C_0$:

$$C(x,t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n \cos\!\left[ \frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L} \right] e^{-D(2n+1)^{2} \pi^{2} t / 4L^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Expand $C_0$ as a Fourier cosine series on $[-L, L]$. The odd-mode projection yields:

$$A_n = \frac{4 C_0}{(2n+1)\pi} (-1)^{n}$$

**Step 5:** Total mass remaining in slab at time $t$:

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{1}{2L\,C_0}\int_{-L}^{L} C(x,t)\,dx = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^{2}} \exp\!\left[ -D (2n+1)^{2} \pi^{2} t / 4L^{2} \right]$$

Rearranging for the **cumulative fraction released**:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^{2}} \exp\!\left[ -D (2n+1)^{2} \pi^{2} t \,/\, 4L^{2} \right]\;}$$

**Step 6:** **Short-time limit** ($D t / L^{2} \ll 1$). Retain only the first eigenmode and Taylor-expand the exponential inside the integral of mass. After the standard manipulations (see Crank, *Mathematics of Diffusion*):

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx 2\sqrt{\frac{D\,t}{\pi L^{2}}} \quad (M_t/M_\infty \leq 0.6)$$

This is the famous **$\sqrt{t}$ law** for Fickian leaching. A plot of $M_t/M_\infty$ vs. $\sqrt{t}$ is linear in this regime.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark problem)

**Problem:** A poly(HEMA) hydrogel slab ($L = 1\ \mathbf{mm}$ thick, both faces exposed) is loaded with $C_0 = 5\ \mathbf{mg/cm^{3}}$ of a model drug. The diffusion coefficient of the drug in the swollen gel is $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-12}\ \mathbf{m^{2}/s}$. Compute:
(a) The cumulative fraction released after **24 hours** using the Fickian series solution.
(b) The leaching flux at steady state, assuming partition coefficient $K = 0.8$.

**Given:** $L = 1 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathbf{m}$, $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-12}\ \mathbf{m^{2}/s}$, $t = 86400\ \mathbf{s}$, $C_0 = 5\ \mathbf{mg/cm^{3}} = 5 \times 10^{3}\ \mathbf{mg/L}$, $K = 0.8$.

### Part (a): Cumulative release after 24 h

**Step 1:** Compute the dimensionless time $\tau = D t / L^{2}$:

$$\tau = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-12})(86400)}{(1 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} = \frac{8.64 \times 10^{-8}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.0864$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the series. For $n = 0$:

$$\exp\!\left[ -\pi^{2} \tau \right] = \exp\!\left[ -9.8696 \times 0.0864 \right] = \exp(-0.8527) = 0.4263$$

For $n = 1$:

$$\frac{1}{9}\exp[-9\pi^{2}\tau] = \frac{1}{9}\exp(-7.674) = \frac{1}{9}(4.61 \times 10^{-4}) = 5.12 \times 10^{-5}$$

For $n = 2$:

$$\frac{1}{25}\exp[-25\pi^{2}\tau] \approx 0 \text{ (negligible)}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the series:

$$\sum = \frac{1}{1}(0.4263) + \frac{1}{9}(4.61 \times 10^{-4}) + \cdots \approx 0.4263$$

**Step 4:** Apply the boxed formula:

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^{2}}(0.4263) = 1 - \frac{8 \times 0.4263}{9.8696} = 1 - 0.3456 = 0.6544$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{M_t/M_\infty \approx 0.654 \text{ or } 65.4\% \text{ released in 24 h}}$

**Step 5 — Cross-check with short-time $\sqrt{t}$ law** (note: 65% is above the strict 60% limit, but as a sanity check):

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx 2\sqrt{\frac{0.0864}{\pi}} = 2\sqrt{0.02750} = 2(0.1658) = 0.3316 \quad (\approx 33\%)$$

The short-time law underestimates at 24 h because the higher-order modes have begun to contribute. The **series solution is the correct answer** for board-exam purposes.

### Part (b): Steady-state leaching flux

**Step 6:** Apply the boxed flux formula:

$$J = \frac{D\,K\,C_0}{L} = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-12})(0.8)(5 \times 10^{3}\,\mathbf{g/m^{3}})}{1 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathbf{m}}$$

$$J = \frac{4 \times 10^{-9}\,\mathbf{g/m^{2}/s}}{10^{-3}\,\mathbf{m}} = 4 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathbf{g \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{J = 4 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathbf{g \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}} \text{ (or } 4\ \mu g\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}\text{)}}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swelling Mechanism — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dry Crosslinked Polymer Network] --> B[Contact with Physiological Fluid]
    B --> C{Three Competing Forces}
    C --> D[Mixing Free Energy - DG mix]
    C --> E[Elastic Retractile Force - DG el]
    C --> F[Ionic Osmotic Pressure - DG ion]
    D --> G[Osmotic Solvent Influx]
    E --> H[Network resists expansion]
    F --> I[Counter-ion swelling]
    G --> J[Equilibrium Swollen State]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Applications: Hydrogel lens, Drug release, Wound dressing]
```

## 4.2 Leaching Pathway — Mass-Transfer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Polymer Matrix with Embedded Species] --> Q[Concentration Gradient dC/dx]
    Q --> R[Surface Boundary Layer]
    R --> S[External Physiological Medium]
    P --> T[Diffusion within polymer D polymer]
    R --> U[Partition coefficient K]
    S --> V[Convective washout / sink]
    T --> W[Leaching Flux J = D K C0 / L]
    U --> W
    W --> X[Clinical Effect: Drug release or Toxicity]
```

## 4.3 Swelling Kinetics Decision Tree (Peppas n-Value)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Measure M_t vs t Data] --> S1[Plot ln M_t / M_inf vs ln t]
    S1 --> S2{Linear Slope = n}
    S2 -->|n = 0.50| S3[Fickian Case I Diffusion]
    S2 -->|0.50 to 1.0| S4[Anomalous Non-Fickian]
    S2 -->|n = 1.0| S5[Case II Relaxation Controlled]
    S2 -->|n greater than 1.0| S6[Super Case II Stress Cracking]
    S3 --> S7[Slab n=0.5, Cylinder n=0.45, Sphere n=0.43]
    S4 --> S8[Both diffusion and chain relaxation active]
    S5 --> S9[Polymer Tg near experimental T]
    S6 --> S10[Front-advancing stress fracture]
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Block Diagram of a Hydrogel Drug-Delivery System (Swelling + Leaching coupled)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Matrix
    A1[Polymer Network Crosslinked] --> A2[Trapped Drug Molecules]
    end
    subgraph Interface
    A2 --> B1[Swelling Front Water Influx]
    A2 --> B2[Drug Diffusion Outward]
    end
    subgraph Bioenvironment
    B1 --> C1[Hydration of network]
    B2 --> C2[Drug release to tissue]
    end
    C1 --> D1[Increased mesh size]
    C1 --> D2[Enhanced D effective]
    D1 --> B2
    D2 --> B2
    C2 --> E[Therapeutic Dose at Target Site]
```

## 4.5 Leaching Profile (Concentration vs. Position in Slab) — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph t0
    A0[Concentration uniform C0 across -L to +L] --> A1[Flat profile at t=0]
    end
    subgraph t1
    B0[Concentration parabolic drop near surfaces] --> B1[Curved profile at t greater than 0]
    end
    subgraph t_inf
    C0[Concentration near zero everywhere] --> C1[Flat near zero at t infinite]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the diagram uses block architecture:** Physical microstructure of a polymer network is too detailed to render in Mermaid. The block diagram above captures the **logical coupling** between solvent ingress (swelling) and solute egress (leaching) in a hydrogel drug-delivery device — a KTU-favoured representation for board answers when a physical sketch is impractical.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Recall)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
**Define the term "swelling ratio" as applied to a polymeric biomaterial. Distinguish between mass swelling ratio ($q_m$) and volume swelling ratio ($q_v$).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Swelling ratio quantifies the solvent uptake capacity of a crosslinked polymer network. The **mass swelling ratio** $q_m$ is defined as the mass of solvent absorbed per unit mass of dry polymer:
$$q_m = \frac{m_s - m_d}{m_d}$$
The **volume swelling ratio** $q_v$ is defined as the volume of solvent absorbed per unit volume of dry polymer:
$$q_v = \frac{V_s - V_d}{V_d}$$
The two are related by the densities of the polymer ($\rho_p$) and the solvent ($\rho_s$) through $q_v = 1 + (\rho_p/\rho_s)(q_m - 1)$. In the Flory–Rehner theory, $q_v$ is used in preference to $q_m$ because volume fractions are the natural variables of the lattice model. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**What is leaching in the context of biomaterials? List two examples each of (i) undesirable leaching and (ii) desirable leaching.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Leaching is the diffusion-controlled release of soluble species (unreacted monomers, additives, oligomers, or encapsulated drugs) from a bulk biomaterial into the surrounding physiological environment, driven by a concentration gradient and governed by Fick's laws. **(1 mark)**
- **Undesirable leaching:** (a) residual methyl methacrylate monomer leaching from PMMA bone cement causing tissue irritation; (b) plasticizer (e.g., DEHP) leaching from PVC tubing used in blood-contact applications. **(1 mark)**
- **Desirable leaching:** (a) controlled release of an antibiotic (e.g., gentamicin) from a PMMA bone-cement bead for treating osteomyelitis; (b) sustained release of an anti-glaucoma drug (e.g., timolol) from a contact-lens hydrogel. **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With the aid of a free-energy balance, derive the **Flory–Rehner equation** for the equilibrium swelling of a neutral, crosslinked polymer network in a thermodynamically good solvent. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A poly(HEMA) contact-lens hydrogel has the following properties: dry mass $m_d = 25\ \mathbf{mg}$; swollen mass in saline at 37 °C, $m_s = 80\ \mathbf{mg}$; density of dry polymer $\rho_p = 1.27\ \mathbf{g/cm^{3}}$; density of saline $\rho_s = 1.00\ \mathbf{g/cm^{3}}$; Flory–Huggins parameter $\chi = 0.45$. Using the Flory–Rehner equation, calculate the **effective crosslink density $n$** and the **molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$**. Molar volume of water $V_1 = 18\ \mathbf{cm^{3}/mol}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks

1. **State the equilibrium condition (1 mark):** At swelling equilibrium, the chemical potential of solvent inside the network equals that outside; equivalently, the derivative of the total free energy with respect to solvent moles vanishes:
$$\frac{\partial \Delta G_{total}}{\partial n_1} = 0, \quad \Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_{mix} + \Delta G_{el} \;(\text{neutral gel, so } \Delta G_{ion} = 0)$$

2. **Write Flory–Huggins mixing free energy per unit volume (2 marks):**
$$\frac{\Delta G_{mix}}{V} = \frac{RT}{V_1}\!\left[ (1 - v_2)\ln(1 - v_2) + \chi\, v_2(1 - v_2) \right]$$

3. **Write elastic free energy of phantom network (2 marks):**
$$\frac{\Delta G_{el}}{V_0} = \tfrac{3}{2} n k_B T \left( v_{2,s}^{\,1/3} - v_{2,s} \right)$$

4. **Differentiate, set equal to zero, and rearrange to obtain the Flory–Rehner equation (2 marks):**
$$-\ln(1 - v_{2,s}) + v_{2,s} + \chi v_{2,s}^{\,2} = V_1 n \left( v_{2,s}^{\,1/3} - \tfrac{v_{2,s}}{2} \right)$$

**Assumptions:** (i) neutral (non-ionic) network, (ii) affine network deformation, (iii) phantom network behaviour (no chain entanglements), (iv) constant temperature and pressure, (v) solvent is a good solvent for the polymer, (vi) volume additivity on mixing. *(State any 4 for full marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Compute $q_m$ (1 mark)**
$$q_m = \frac{m_s - m_d}{m_d} = \frac{80 - 25}{25} = \frac{55}{25} = 2.20$$

**Step 2: Convert to $q_v$ (1 mark)**
$$q_v = 1 + \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_s}(q_m - 1) = 1 + \frac{1.27}{1.00}(2.20 - 1) = 1 + 1.27(1.20) = 1 + 1.524 = 2.524$$

**Step 3: Compute $v_{2,s} = 1/q_v$ (0.5 marks)**
$$v_{2,s} = \frac{1}{2.524} = 0.3962$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the LHS (mixing term) (1.5 marks)**
$$-\ln(1 - 0.3962) + 0.3962 + (0.45)(0.3962)^{2}$$
$$= -\ln(0.6038) + 0.3962 + 0.45(0.1569)$$
$$= 0.5044 + 0.3962 + 0.0706 = 0.9712$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the RHS factor and solve for $n$ (2 marks)**
$$\text{RHS factor} = v_{2,s}^{\,1/3} - \tfrac{v_{2,s}}{2} = (0.3962)^{1/3} - \tfrac{0.3962}{2} = 0.7340 - 0.1981 = 0.5359$$
$$n = \frac{0.9712}{V_1 \times 0.5359} = \frac{0.9712}{18 \times 0.5359} = \frac{0.9712}{9.6462} = 0.1007\ \mathbf{mol/m^{3}}$$

**Step 6: Compute $M_c$ (1 mark)**
$$M_c = \frac{\rho_p}{n} = \frac{1.27 \times 10^{6}\ \mathbf{g/m^{3}}}{0.1007\ \mathbf{mol/m^{3}}} \approx 1.262 \times 10^{7}\ \mathbf{g/mol}$$

> **Final Answers:** $\boxed{n \approx 0.10\ \mathbf{mol/m^{3}}, \quad M_c \approx 1.26 \times 10^{7}\ \mathbf{g/mol}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students report $q_m$ as the swollen-to-dry mass ratio ($80/25 = 3.2$) instead of the **solvent-to-dry** ratio ($2.20$). This single sign error propagates and yields a $q_v$ that is **too high**, an $n$ that is too low, and an $M_c$ that is unrealistically large. The formula is $q_m = (m_s - m_d)/m_d$, **NOT** $m_s/m_d$. **Lose 1 mark per downstream variable if committed.** Also, do not forget to **convert units consistently** (cm³/mol vs. m³/mol); 1 cm³/mol = 10⁻⁶ m³/mol. A unit slip in $V_1$ yields an $M_c$ off by 10⁶.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of leaching** from a planar biomaterial slab loaded with a diffusible drug, clearly stating Fick's laws. Show that for short times, the cumulative fraction released follows the **$\sqrt{t}$ law**, i.e. $M_t/M_\infty = 2\sqrt{D t / (\pi L^{2})}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A drug-eluting stent coating is modelled as a planar slab of half-thickness $L = 50\ \mathbf{\mu m}$, with uniform initial drug loading $C_0 = 100\ \mathbf{mg/cm^{3}}$ and diffusion coefficient $D = 5.0 \times 10^{-14}\ \mathbf{m^{2}/s}$. The device is immersed in a perfect sink. Determine:
(i) The cumulative fraction released after **30 days**.
(ii) The time required to release **80%** of the loaded drug.
(iii) The steady-state leaching flux $J$ assuming partition coefficient $K = 0.6$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks

1. **State Fick's laws (2 marks):**
   - **Fick's first law (steady state):** $J = -D \partial C/\partial x$
   - **Fick's second law (transient):** $\partial C/\partial t = D \partial^{2} C/\partial x^{2}$

2. **State the problem setup (1 mark):** Planar slab, $-L \leq x \leq L$, initial condition $C(x, 0) = C_0$, sink boundary conditions $C(\pm L, t) = 0$. Apply separation of variables to obtain eigenmode series:
$$C(x,t) = \frac{4 C_0}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n}}{2n+1}\cos\!\left[ \frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L} \right] e^{-D(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}t/4L^{2}}$$

3. **Integrate to find $M_t$ (1 mark):**
$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^{2}}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^{2}}\exp\!\left[ -\frac{D(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2} t}{4L^{2}} \right]$$

4. **Derive the short-time limit (3 marks):** When $D t/L^{2} \ll 1$, the integrand is appreciable only near the surface, and the error-function solution applies. The standard short-time asymptotic expansion of the Crank series (or direct integration of the Gaussian approximation of the concentration profile) yields:
$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 2\sqrt{\frac{D\,t}{\pi L^{2}}}$$
This is the **$\sqrt{t}$ law**. The full derivation involves recognizing that the dominant contribution to mass loss comes from the near-surface region $x \approx L$, where the complementary error function approximation $C/C_0 \approx \mathrm{erfc}[(L-x)/2\sqrt{Dt}]$ is valid.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $L = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathbf{m} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathbf{m}$, $D = 5.0 \times 10^{-14}\ \mathbf{m^{2}/s}$, $t_1 = 30 \times 86400 = 2.592 \times 10^{6}\ \mathbf{s}$, $C_0 = 100 \times 10^{3}\ \mathbf{g/m^{3}} = 1.0 \times 10^{5}\ \mathbf{g/m^{3}}$, $K = 0.6$.

**Part (i): Cumulative release at 30 days (3 marks)**

**Step 1:** Dimensionless time
$$\tau = \frac{D t_1}{L^{2}} = \frac{(5.0 \times 10^{-14})(2.592 \times 10^{6})}{(5.0 \times 10^{-5})^{2}} = \frac{1.296 \times 10^{-7}}{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 51.84$$

**Step 2:** First exponential term ($n=0$):
$$\exp(-\pi^{2} \tau) = \exp(-9.8696 \times 51.84) = \exp(-511.6) \approx 0$$

All higher terms are even smaller. Therefore the series sum → 0, and:
$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - 0 = 1.00 \quad (100\%)$$

**Result:** $\boxed{M_t/M_\infty \approx 1.00 \text{ (essentially complete release at 30 days)}}$

> Interpretation: The characteristic diffusion time $\tau_c = L^{2}/D = (2.5 \times 10^{-9})/(5.0 \times 10^{-14}) = 5.0 \times 10^{4}\ \mathbf{s} \approx 13.9\ \mathbf{h}$. After 30 days, the system is many hundreds of characteristic times past equilibrium, so the device is fully depleted. This is typical of thin polymer coatings.

**Part (ii): Time to release 80% (2 marks)**

**Step 1:** Set $M_t/M_\infty = 0.80$ in the series. The series converges quickly, so retain only $n=0$:

$$0.80 = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} e^{-\pi^{2} D t / 4L^{2}}$$

$$e^{-\pi^{2} D t / 4L^{2}} = \frac{0.20 \pi^{2}}{8} = \frac{0.20 \times 9.8696}{8} = 0.2467$$

$$-\frac{\pi^{2} D t}{4L^{2}} = \ln(0.2467) = -1.398$$

$$t = \frac{1.398 \times 4 L^{2}}{\pi^{2} D} = \frac{1.398 \times 4 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-9})}{9.8696 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-14}}$$

$$t = \frac{1.398 \times 10^{-8}}{4.935 \times 10^{-13}} = 2.834 \times 10^{4}\ \mathbf{s} \approx 7.87\ \mathbf{h}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{t_{80\%} \approx 7.9\ \mathbf{hours}}$

**Part (iii): Steady-state flux (2 marks)**

$$J = \frac{D K C_0}{L} = \frac{(5.0 \times 10^{-14})(0.6)(1.0 \times 10^{5})}{5.0 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$J = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-9}}{5.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathbf{g\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{J = 6.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathbf{g\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}} = 60\ \mu g\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part i):** A common mistake is to plug $t = 30$ days into the **short-time** $\sqrt{t}$ formula. At $\tau = 51.8$, we are **deeply in the long-time regime** ($M_t/M_\infty \to 1$). Using the short-time formula gives $M_t/M_\infty = 2\sqrt{51.8/\pi} = 8.12$, which is **physically impossible** (cannot exceed 1). Students must identify the regime first: compute $\tau$ and check magnitude, then select the correct formula. **Lose 2 marks** for using the wrong asymptotic form. Additionally, do not forget to convert $L = 50\ \mu m$ to metres ($5 \times 10^{-5}\ m$); a factor of $10^{6}$ error in $L^{2}$ propagates to a $10^{6}$ error in the time. **Lose 1 mark per downstream variable.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Swelling = solvent in; Leaching = solute out** — opposite directions, both diffusion-controlled. (KTU favourite definition contrast.)
- **Flory–Rehner equation** is THE high-yield formula: $\;-\ln(1-v_{2,s}) + v_{2,s} + \chi v_{2,s}^{2} = V_1 n (v_{2,s}^{1/3} - v_{2,s}/2)\;$.
- $q_m = (m_s - m_d)/m_d$ — solvent-to-dry, **NOT** swollen-to-dry.
- $q_v = 1 + (\rho_p/\rho_s)(q_m - 1)$ — required to convert to the volume fraction $v_{2,s} = 1/q_v$.
- Mesh size $\xi$ controls solute diffusion; larger $\xi \Rightarrow$ faster leaching, faster swelling.
- **Peppas exponent $n$**: 0.5 (slab) / 0.45 (cylinder) / 0.43 (sphere) → Fickian.
- **$\sqrt{t}$ law** valid only for $M_t/M_\infty \leq 0.6$; for $>0.6$, use full Crank series.
- Fick's first law gives steady-state flux: $J = DKC_0/L$.
- For thin coatings (stents, contact lenses), $\tau = Dt/L^{2}$ becomes large very quickly → full release.
- **Leaching ≠ degradation**: leaching is physical diffusion of an intact solute; degradation is bond cleavage.
- **Polyelectrolyte gels** (alginate, PAA) add a $\Delta G_{ion}$ term due to counter-ion osmotic pressure.
- **Toxic leachables** (residual monomers, initiators) are regulated by **ISO 10993** biocompatibility standards.
- Common KTU examiners' traps: unit conversions (cm³ ↔ m³, μm ↔ m), wrong sign in $q_m$, misidentification of geometry in Peppas exponent, using short-time $\sqrt{t}$ formula outside its range of validity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion and dissolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT412 - BIOMATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion and dissolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Corrosion and Dissolution of Biomaterials

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion** is defined as the irreversible deterioration of a metallic biomaterial caused by an electrochemical reaction with its surrounding physiological environment, leading to the release of metal ions, loss of structural integrity, and potential adverse biological responses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dissolution** refers to the process by which ions, atoms, or molecules from a biomaterial (metallic, ceramic, or polymeric) detach and migrate into the surrounding tissue or physiological medium, either through chemical reaction (corrosion) or simple solvation/leaching without electron transfer.

In the human body, an implant is exposed to a warm (~37°C), oxygenated, chloride-rich aqueous electrolyte (pH ≈ 7.4) that is aggressively corrosive to most engineering metals. Hence, **corrosion behaviour is the single most critical degradation mechanism for load-bearing orthopaedic and dental implants**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a steel nail left in a saltwater bucket: it slowly rusts because the salt water acts as an electrolyte, allowing electrons to flow between anodic (metal-dissolving) and cathodic (reduction) sites. The human body is essentially that bucket — a **7 litre saline electrolyte bath** at 37°C.

| Body Environment | Approximate Value | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| pH of blood plasma | **7.35 – 7.45** | Near-neutral aqueous electrolyte |
| Chloride ion concentration | **~ 113 mEq/L** | Stronger than seawater (~ 540 mEq/L NaCl, but Cl⁻ dominates) |
| Dissolved oxygen | **~ 40 – 100 mmHg** | Aerated electrolyte |
| Temperature | **36.5 – 37.5 °C** | Warm corrosive bath |

**Plain English Summary:** When a metal implant is placed in the body, the saline blood acts like a battery acid (mild version), slowly eating away the metal. This releases ions (e.g., Ni²⁺, Cr³⁺, Co²⁺) into the tissue, which can cause toxicity, allergy, or implant loosening.

### 1.3 Why It Matters for KTU

Corrosion directly determines:
1. **Implant service life** (typically expected ≥ 15–20 years for orthopaedic implants).
2. **Biocompatibility** — released ions are cytotoxic/allergenic.
3. **Mechanical failure** — pitting and crevice corrosion act as crack initiation sites.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently frame 14-mark questions around "Discuss the various types of corrosion encountered in metallic implants with preventive measures." Memorise the **8 types** and **2 mitigation strategies** in module 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrochemical Basis of Corrosion

Every corrosion process in aqueous medium is an **electrochemical cell** with:
- **Anode:** Site of oxidation (metal dissolution)
$$\text{M} \longrightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^-$$
- **Cathode:** Site of reduction (most often oxygen or proton reduction)
$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^- \quad \text{(aerobic)}$$
$$2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(anaerobic)}$$

The two half-cells are connected by:
1. **Electron flow** through the metal (internal circuit).
2. **Ion flow** through the electrolyte (external circuit).

The driving force is the **electrode potential difference** (ΔE) between the anodic and cathodic reactions.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Tendency — The Nernst Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium electrode potential of a half-cell as a function of ion concentration and temperature. It tells us **whether** corrosion is thermodynamically possible, **not** the rate.

For the general half-reaction:
$$\text{Ox} + n e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$$

The Nernst equation is:
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{\text{Red}}}{a_{\text{Ox}}}$$

At the **standard physiological temperature of 37 °C (310 K)**, and converting natural log to base-10:
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0615}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]} \quad \text{(V vs. SHE, 37 °C)}$$

**Symbols and Units (KTU-Critical):**
- $E$ = Electrode potential under non-standard conditions (V)
- $E^\circ$ = Standard electrode potential (V)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96 485 C·mol⁻¹** (≈ 96 500 C·mol⁻¹)
- $a$ = Activity of species (≈ concentration for dilute solutions)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Law | Formula | Key Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst equation (general) | $E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Predict thermodynamic tendency |
| 2 | Nernst at 37 °C | $E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0615}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Body-temperature applications |
| 3 | Nernst at 25 °C | $E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Standard lab reference |
| 4 | Faraday's corrosion rate | $r = \frac{I \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot A}$ | Calculate mass loss / mm per year |
| 5 | Mixed-potential form (corrosion current via Tafel) | $\eta = \pm \beta \log \frac{\vert i \vert}{i_0}$ | Polarisation studies |
| 6 | Evans diagram condition | $E_{\text{cathode}} = E_{\text{anode}} = E_{\text{corr}}$ | Define $E_{\text{corr}}$ and $i_{\text{corr}}$ |
| 7 | pH–potential (Pourbaix) bands | pH = $\frac{E - 1.23}{0.0592} \times (-n/4) + \text{const.}$ | Predict Immunity / Passivity / Corrosion |
| 8 | Helmholtz double-layer capacitance | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Surface film behaviour |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol "$\vert i \vert$" denotes the **magnitude** of current density and uses the LaTeX `\vert` command so that the markdown table parser is not broken.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Orthopaedics** | Hip stems (Ti-6Al-4V), knee joints (Co-Cr) — must resist ~ 20 yrs of cyclic loading in saline. |
| **Dentistry** | Amalgam fillings, Ti abutments — pH drops to 4–5 after sugar exposure. |
| **Cardiovascular** | Stents (316L SS, L605 Co-Cr) — must survive ~ 10⁸ heart-beat cycles in blood. |
| **Surgical Instruments** | Reusable scissors/forceps — passivation via nitric acid bath. |
| **Tissue Engineering Scaffolds** | Mg-based biodegradable stents — controlled dissolution is *desired*. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation

The Nernst equation is derived from the **Gibbs free energy change** of an electrochemical cell.

**Step 1 — Start with the fundamental thermodynamic relation.**
At constant temperature and pressure, the maximum non-expansion work obtainable equals the change in Gibbs free energy:
$$\Delta G = -n F E_{\text{cell}}$$

**Step 2 — Express ΔG in terms of the reaction quotient $Q$.**
For any non-standard reaction, the Gibbs energy is:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the standard potential relation $\Delta G^\circ = -n F E^\circ$.**
$$-n F E = -n F E^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E$.**
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 5 — Convert $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$.**
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the constant at body temperature $T = 310\ \text{K}$.**
$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96\,485} = 0.0615\ \text{V}$$

**Step 7 — Final form used in KTU problems.**
$$\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0615}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]} \quad \text{(V vs. SHE)}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Faraday's Corrosion Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Faraday Law of Electrolysis** is the foundation for converting measured corrosion current into a meaningful engineering rate (mm/year, mpy). KTU examiners love this derivation.

**Given:** A Ti-6Al-4V implant experiences a corrosion current $I = 25\ \mu\text{A}$ over a surface area $A = 30\ \text{cm}^2$. Titanium dissolves as Ti⁴⁺, so $n = 4$, and $M_{\text{Ti}} = 47.87\ \text{g/mol}$, density $\rho = 4.51\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

**Step 1 — Write Faraday's mass-loss equation.**
$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Express the corrosion rate in mass per unit area per unit time.**
$$r = \frac{m}{A \cdot t} = \frac{I \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**
$$r = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-6}) \times 47.87}{4 \times 96\,485 \times 30 \times 10^{-4}}\ \text{g·cm}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator and denominator separately.**
- Numerator: $25 \times 10^{-6} \times 47.87 = 1.197 \times 10^{-3}$
- Denominator: $4 \times 96\,485 \times 30 \times 10^{-4} = 1157.82$

$$r = \frac{1.197 \times 10^{-3}}{1157.82} = 1.034 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{g·cm}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mm/year (engineering unit).**
Use $1\ \text{g·cm}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1} = 3.1536 \times 10^{7}\ \text{mm/year}$ (after dividing by density in g/cm³ and multiplying by 10 for cm→mm and $3.154\times 10^{7}$ s/year):

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{\text{linear}} &= \frac{r}{\rho} \times \frac{10\ \text{mm}}{1\ \text{cm}} \times 3.1536 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s/year} \\
&= \frac{1.034 \times 10^{-6}}{4.51} \times 10 \times 3.1536 \times 10^{7} \\
&= 7.23 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mm/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final answer in mpy (mils per year, where 1 mm = 39.37 mil).**
$$r = 7.23 \times 10^{-5} \times 39.37 = 2.85 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mpy}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Anything below **5 mpy** is considered **"very good corrosion resistance"** by ASTM G48 / F2129 standards — typical for passivated Ti alloys.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Implant Alloys

| Property | 316L Stainless Steel | Co-Cr-Mo (ASTM F75) | Ti-6Al-4V (ASTM F136) | Pure Ti (Grade 4) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 7.9 | 8.3 | 4.5 | 4.5 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 200 | 220 | 110 | 105 |
| $E^\circ$ (V vs SHE, most active) | $-0.05$ (Fe) | $-0.28$ (Co) | $-1.63$ (Ti) | $-1.63$ (Ti) |
| Passive oxide | Cr₂O₃ (weak) | Cr₂O₃ (medium) | TiO₂ (very stable) | TiO₂ (very stable) |
| $i_{\text{corr}}$ (μA/cm²) in HBSS | 0.1 – 1 | 0.05 – 0.5 | 0.01 – 0.1 | 0.005 – 0.05 |
| Typical use | Temporary fixation, stents | Knee, dental | Hip stems, spinal | Dental implants, screws |
| Major corrosion concern | Crevice, pitting in Cl⁻ | Galvanic coupling | Fretting at modular junctions | Hydrogen embrittlement |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechanism of Electrochemical Corrosion — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metallic Implant in Body] --> B{Surface in Contact with Electrolyte}
    B -->|Anodic site| C[Mn dissolves to Mn+ plus n electrons]
    B -->|Cathodic site| D[O2 reduced to OH minus or H plus reduced to H2]
    C --> E[Electron flow through metal]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Current completes through ionic conduction in tissue fluid]
    F --> G[Metal ions released into tissue]
    G --> H[Biomechanical effects]
    H --> H1[Local inflammation and toxicity]
    H --> H2[Implant loosening and pitting cracks]
    H --> H3[Allergic or carcinogenic response]
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Corrosion Types (KTU-Exam Ready)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Types of Corrosion in Biomaterials] --> B[Uniform or General]
    A --> C[Localized]
    A --> D[Mechanically Assisted]
    A --> E[Environmentally Assisted]
    B --> B1[Even attack across surface]
    C --> C1[Pitting]
    C --> C2[Crevice]
    C --> C3[Galvanic]
    C --> C4[Intergranular]
    D --> D1[Stress Corrosion Cracking]
    D --> D2[Corrosion Fatigue]
    D --> D3[Fretting Corrosion]
    E --> E1[Hydrogen Embrittlement]
    E --> E2[Microbial Induced]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Passivation and Pourbaix Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pourbaix Diagram of Metal M in Water] --> P1[E above passivation line]
    P --> P2[E between passivation and immunity lines]
    P --> P3[E below immunity line]
    P1 --> R1[Corrosion region Metal dissolves as M to the power of n plus]
    P2 --> R2[Passive region Stable oxide M2On protects surface]
    P3 --> R3[Immune region No thermodynamic driving force for reaction]
    R2 --> R4[This is the desired zone for implants]
    R2 --> R5[e.g. TiO2 on Ti, Cr2O3 on Co-Cr]
```

## 4.4 Pourbaix Diagram Conceptual Sketch (E vs pH)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Pourbaix_For_Implant_Metal
        direction TB
        HIGH_E[High E: Passive / Corrosion zone]:::passive
        MID_E[Mid E: Passive zone - target for implants]:::target
        LOW_E[Low E: Immune zone]:::immune
        ACID[Acidic pH < 4]:::acid
        BODY[Body pH 7.4]:::body
        ALK[Alkaline pH > 9]:::alk
    end
    classDef passive fill:#fdd,stroke:#900,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#cfc,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef immune fill:#cdf,stroke:#006,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef acid fill:#eee,stroke:#444,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef body fill:#ffe,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef alk fill:#eee,stroke:#444,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four Pourbaix domains every KTU student must know:
> 1. **Immunity** – metal is thermodynamically stable (e.g., Au, Pt).
> 2. **Corrosion** – metal dissolves (e.g., active Fe).
> 3. **Passivation** – stable oxide film protects (e.g., TiO₂, Cr₂O₃, Al₂O₃).
> 4. **Hydrogen evolution** – at very low E, H⁺ reduces to H₂.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`
**Differentiate between corrosion and dissolution in the context of biomaterials.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Corrosion** is an *electrochemical* degradation process in which a metal oxidises (loses electrons) and the released ions migrate into the surrounding electrolyte, accompanied by a complementary reduction reaction (e.g., oxygen reduction). It applies strictly to **metallic** biomaterials and requires an ionically conducting medium such as body fluid. (1.5 marks)
> - **Dissolution** is a broader term that includes the release of ions, atoms, or molecules from **any** biomaterial (metallic, ceramic, polymeric) into the surrounding medium. It may occur by simple solvation, ion exchange, or chemical reaction, and **does not necessarily involve electron transfer**. (1.5 marks)
> - Example: 316L stainless steel corroding in saline (electrochemical, releases Fe²⁺, Cr³⁺, Ni²⁺) is corrosion. A bioceramic such as tricalcium phosphate releasing Ca²⁺ and PO₄³⁻ ions into surrounding bone is dissolution. (bonus key point)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**What is pitting corrosion? Why is it particularly dangerous for orthopaedic implants?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Pitting corrosion** is a form of localised attack in which small cavities or "pits" form on the metal surface, typically in chloride-rich environments such as physiological saline. The pits act as fixed anodes while the surrounding surface becomes the cathode, producing a high local current density. (1.5 marks)
> - It is dangerous because: (a) the small pits act as **stress concentrators** and crack-initiation sites under cyclic load, (b) the corrosion rate within a pit can be **1000× the average** surface rate, (c) it often proceeds *underneath* the passive oxide, so it is **not visible** until the implant suddenly fails, and (d) metal ions released from pits can cause **localised inflammation and bone resorption**, leading to aseptic loosening. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern (ESE):** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Provide model answers in full.

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **electrochemical mechanism of corrosion** of a metallic implant in the physiological environment, with the help of the relevant half-cell reactions. Discuss the role of the **Nernst equation** in determining the thermodynamic tendency for corrosion of an implant material.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Definition of the corrosive environment** (1 mark): Body fluid is an aerated, neutral, chloride-rich electrolyte at 37 °C; the implant acts as a heterogeneous electrode.

2. **Anodic reaction** (1 mark): Metal oxidation,
   $$\text{M} \longrightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^-$$
   (e.g., for Fe: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$)

3. **Cathodic reaction** (1 mark): The dominant cathodic process in aerated saline is oxygen reduction,
   $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$
   In oxygen-depleted crevices, proton reduction dominates,
   $$2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2$$

4. **Electron and ion transport** (1 mark): Electrons flow through the metal; ions flow through the electrolyte, completing the electrochemical cell.

5. **Role of the Nernst equation** (2 marks):
   $$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0615}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$
   - It predicts the equilibrium electrode potential of each half-cell.
   - A *negative* $E$ for the metal (e.g., $E^\circ_{\text{Fe}} = -0.44$ V) and a *positive* $E$ for O₂/H₂O (≈ +0.82 V at pH 7) yield a **positive cell EMF** ≈ 1.26 V — confirming strong thermodynamic driving force for corrosion of Fe in the body.
   - The Nernst equation is a *thermodynamic* criterion only; it cannot predict **rate**, which requires kinetic tools such as Tafel plots and polarisation curves.

6. **Conclusion** (1 mark): Because $\Delta E > 0$, corrosion is spontaneous for most engineering metals, which is why surface passivation (TiO₂, Cr₂O₃) or noble alloys (Pt, Au, Ta) are mandatory for long-term implants.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **Pourbaix diagram** (E vs pH) for a typical implant metal. Mark the **immunity, corrosion, and passivation regions**, and explain why passivation is exploited in the design of orthopaedic implants such as Ti-6Al-4V.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Definition of a Pourbaix diagram** (1 mark): A Pourbaix diagram plots the equilibrium electrode potential $E$ (V) on the y-axis versus the pH of the electrolyte on the x-axis, at a given temperature and ion concentration. It is constructed from the Nernst equation applied to all possible equilibria of the metal–water system.

2. **Sketch description** (1.5 marks): The student is expected to describe (and ideally draw) three diagonal/horizontal lines:
   - A horizontal line at low E: separating **immune** (metallic) and **corrosion** (ionic) regions.
   - A diagonal line (slope ≈ −0.0592 V/pH) separating **corrosion** and **passive** (oxide) regions.
   - A horizontal/curved line at high pH separating **passive** from soluble hydroxo-complexes.

3. **Three regions — immunity, corrosion, passivation** (1.5 marks):
   - **Immunity** (low E): the metal is thermodynamically stable, e.g., Au, Pt, Ta.
   - **Corrosion** (mid E, away from passive line): metal dissolves as Mⁿ⁺, e.g., active Fe in aerated saline.
   - **Passivation** (high E, but not too low): a stable oxide/hydroxide film covers the surface, slowing ion transport drastically.

4. **Ti-6Al-4V example** (2 marks): At pH ≈ 7.4 and E ≈ −0.05 V (typical for an implant coupled to surrounding tissue), Ti-6Al-4V lies well **inside the TiO₂ passivation region**. TiO₂ is a wide-bandgap (~3 eV) n-type semiconductor that is chemically inert, biocompatible, and self-healing in oxygenated aqueous media. The oxide is only a few nm thick yet reduces the corrosion current density $i_{\text{corr}}$ by 4–6 orders of magnitude.

5. **Design implication** (1 mark): Orthopaedic implants are therefore deliberately manufactured (and surface-treated by anodising, chemical oxidation, or grit-blasting) to ensure the in-service E–pH point remains within the passive domain for the lifetime of the device.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Classify and briefly describe the **various types of corrosion** encountered in metallic biomaterials. Highlight the mechanisms most relevant to orthopaedic and dental implants.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Classification (with one-line mechanism of each — 1 mark per type, 8 types considered; choose any 7 in detail):**

1. **Uniform (General) Corrosion** — Even metal dissolution across the entire exposed surface; rare in implants due to passivation, but seen in non-passive Mg-based biodegradable stents.

2. **Pitting Corrosion** — Localised cavities initiate where the passive film breaks down (often in Cl⁻ environments); pit interiors are acidic (Mⁿ⁺ hydrolysis), accelerating attack.

3. **Crevice Corrosion** — Occurs in shielded gaps (screw-plate interface, modular taper junctions of hip stems); differential aeration and Cl⁻ accumulation cause intense local attack.

4. **Galvanic Corrosion** — When two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact (e.g., a Ti stem with a Co-Cr head in a modular hip), the more active one becomes the anode and corrodes preferentially.

5. **Intergranular Corrosion** — Attack along grain boundaries where Cr-depletion (in 316L SS) or carbide precipitation has occurred during sensitisation welding.

6. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — Combined action of tensile stress and a corrosive medium produces brittle cracks (e.g., stainless steel in Cl⁻ at 37 °C).

7. **Corrosion Fatigue** — Cyclic loading + corrosion = accelerated crack growth; reduces fatigue life below that predicted in air.

8. **Fretting Corrosion** — Micromotion at modular interfaces (e.g., Morse taper of a hip prosthesis) disrupts the passive film and produces corrosive wear debris that provokes osteolysis.

9. **Hydrogen Embrittlement** — Cathodically produced H atoms diffuse into Ti or high-strength SS, causing delayed brittle fracture.

**Most relevant to orthopaedics & dentistry (1 mark):**
- **Fretting, crevice, and pitting** at modular junctions in hip/knee replacements.
- **Galvanic corrosion** in multi-material dental restorations.
- **SCC and corrosion fatigue** in load-bearing plates and screws.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **dissolution behaviour of biodegradable magnesium-based implants**. How does the rate of dissolution influence the mechanical integrity and biological response, and what strategies are used to slow it down?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Why Mg?** (1 mark): Magnesium has a density (1.74 g/cm³) and elastic modulus (~45 GPa) closely matching bone, it is biocompatible, and the recommended daily allowance is ~ 400 mg — so any released Mg²⁺ is metabolically tolerated. The corrosion reaction is,
   $$\text{Mg} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2 + \text{H}_2$$

2. **Mechanism of dissolution** (1 mark): In aqueous Cl⁻, the Mg(OH)₂ film is converted to soluble MgCl₂, exposing fresh metal. Reaction:
   $$\text{Mg} + 2\text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + 2e^-$$
   Hydrogen gas is evolved, which can form subcutaneous bubbles in vivo.

3. **Effect on mechanical integrity** (1.5 marks): Pure Mg corrodes at 1–4 mm/year in simulated body fluid, far too fast for orthopaedic use (the implant would lose load-bearing capacity within weeks). Localised pitting at grain boundaries creates stress concentrators, accelerating fatigue failure.

4. **Biological response** (1.5 marks): Rapid dissolution elevates local pH (alkalosis) and H₂ pressure, which can disrupt surrounding tissue. Conversely, slow and uniform dissolution is desirable for vascular stents (3–6 months) and bone-fixation devices (12–24 months).

5. **Strategies to slow dissolution** (2 marks — list any four with brief justification):
   - **Alloying** with Al, Zn, Mn, Ca, Zr, Y, or rare earths (e.g., WE43, AZ91) to refine grains and form protective intermetallics.
   - **Surface coatings** — biodegradable polymers (PLGA, chitosan), Ca-P coatings (hydroxyapatite, brushite), MgF₂ conversion coatings, and diamond-like carbon.
   - **Anodisation / micro-arc oxidation** to grow a thicker, more stable MgO layer.
   - **Cathodic hydrogenation pre-treatment** or **ion-implantation** to reduce reactivity.
   - **Composite approaches** — Mg reinforced with hydroxyapatite or β-TCP particles.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Module 4 (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Confusing "corrosion" and "dissolution"** in definitions — examiners mark this strictly (½ mark deducted per confusion).
> 2. **Forgetting units** in Nernst-equation substitution — always state T in **K** and F in **C/mol** before plugging into the 0.0615 form.
> 3. **Missing half-reactions** in mechanism questions — *both* anodic and cathodic reactions must be written; partial credit is rare.
> 4. **Not labelling the regions** of a Pourbaix diagram — immunity, corrosion, passivation must all be explicitly named.
> 5. **Confusing mpy (mils per year) with mm/year** — they differ by a factor of 39.37. Always state the unit.
> 6. **Stating that Ti is "biocompatible because it doesn't corrode"** — it does corrode; the protective TiO₂ film makes the **rate** negligible.
> 7. **Omitting Fretting corrosion** when discussing modular hip implants — examiners from orthopaedic modules treat this as essential.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Corrosion = electrochemical degradation; dissolution = ion release (any mechanism).**
- **Nernst equation** gives *thermodynamic tendency*; it never gives the *rate*.
  $$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0615}{n}\log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$
- **Faraday's law** converts corrosion current to mass loss / linear rate.
  $$r = \frac{I M}{n F A}$$
- **Passivation** (TiO₂, Cr₂O₃, Al₂O₃) is the principal engineering strategy for long-term metallic implants.
- **8 key types of corrosion to memorise:** uniform, pitting, crevice, galvanic, intergranular, SCC, corrosion fatigue, fretting, hydrogen embrittlement.
- **Pourbaix diagram** has three regions: immunity, corrosion, passivation.
- **Body environment**: 37 °C, pH 7.4, aerated, Cl⁻-rich — the perfect corrosive electrolyte.
- **Best implant metals by corrosion resistance ranking (best to worst in body):** Ti / Ti alloys > Co-Cr-Mo > 316L SS > Mg-based (intentionally dissolving).
- **Biodegradable Mg implants** need surface engineering to slow H₂ evolution and alkalosis.
- **For KTU numerical problems:** always remember the constant **0.0615 V** at 37 °C (not 0.0592 V, which is for 25 °C).
- **Engineering constant to remember:** Faraday's constant $F = 96\,485$ C·mol⁻¹; 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/year.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick self-test formula card (print and keep):**
> - Nernst: $E = E^\circ - (0.0615/n)\log Q$ at 37 °C
> - Corrosion rate: $r = IM/(nFA)$ g·cm⁻²·s⁻¹
> - Convert to mm/yr: $r_{\text{mm/yr}} = (r/\rho) \times 3.156 \times 10^{8}$
> - Pourbaix: aim for the **passive region** in body pH 7.4.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reaction of biological molecules with biomaterial surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT412 - BIOMATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reaction of biological molecules with biomaterial surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: Reaction of Biological Molecules with Biomaterial Surfaces

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4 Focus):**
> When any biomaterial is implanted in the body, its surface is *instantly* (within milliseconds to seconds) coated by a layer of biological molecules—primarily **water, ions, and proteins**—before any cell ever touches it. Therefore, the *real* surface that cells "see" is **not** the bare biomaterial, but a **bio-modified surface** (a protein corona). Understanding these molecular reactions is mandatory for designing biocompatible implants.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **reaction of biological molecules with biomaterial surfaces** refers to the cascade of physico-chemical and biochemical interactions occurring at the solid–biological interface immediately after a biomaterial contacts physiological fluids (blood, interstitial fluid, saliva, etc.). These reactions include:

1. **Water and ion adsorption** (within nanoseconds).
2. **Protein adsorption and reorganization** (within seconds to minutes).
3. **Conformational changes of adsorbed proteins** (denaturation, exposure of cryptic sites).
4. **Recognition by cells** via integrin–ligand binding.
5. **Enzymatic reactions, biofilm formation, and thrombosis** in longer timescales.

The thermodynamic driving force is governed primarily by the **interfacial free energy (γ)** and **surface energy mismatch** between the biomaterial and the biological medium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The First Tenant Rule":**
> Imagine a newly built, empty hotel (the biomaterial implant) on opening day. Before any guest (cell) can book a room, the doorman (water molecules) arrives first, then the local tour guides (ions and small proteins) set up their welcome desk in the lobby. By the time the actual guests (cells) arrive, the hotel's *original interior design* is completely hidden by banners, flyers, and information desks set up by the welcome committee.
>
> **Translation:** Cells *never* interact with the bare material. They only "see" the layer of adsorbed proteins and water. This is the **bio-interfacial reality** of every implant.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in This Topic

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical Surface Tension | $\gamma_c$ | $\approx 20$–$30$ dyn/cm for fluoropolymers; $>40$ dyn/cm for metals/ceramics | Predicts wettability and protein adhesion tendency |
| Water Contact Angle | $\theta$ | $<90^\circ$ = hydrophilic; $>90^\circ$ = hydrophobic | Quantifies surface wettability |
| Average protein diameter | $d_p$ | $\approx 3$–$15$ nm | Sets the scale of surface coverage |
| Protein diffusion coefficient (in plasma) | $D_p$ | $\approx 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ cm²/s | Governs rate of arrival at surface |
| Plasma protein concentration range | $C_p$ | $\approx 60$–$80$ g/L (albumin dominant) | Determines competitive adsorption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** The most abundant protein (albumin) is NOT the most surface-active. Smaller, less abundant proteins like **fibrinogen and HMWK (High Molecular Weight Kininogen)** are often the first to arrive and stick — this is the basis of the **Vroman Effect**, a frequently asked concept in board examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchical "Race to the Surface"

When an implant enters the body, the following sequence of events occurs — every step is governed by a different timescale and thermodynamic driver:

**Step 1 — Water & Ion Adsorption (0–1 ns)**
- Polar water molecules orient their dipoles based on the surface chemistry of the biomaterial.
- Ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺) arrange into the **electrical double layer** (Helmholtz/Stern/Gouy-Chapman model).
- Driven by minimization of interfacial free energy $\gamma_{SL}$ (solid–liquid).

**Step 2 — Small Molecule / Salt Layer Formation (1 ns – 1 μs)**
- Hydration shell and salt screening are established.
- On hydrophobic surfaces, water forms a *clathrate-like* low-entropy layer — energetically unfavorable, driving subsequent protein displacement.

**Step 3 — Protein Adsorption (1 μs – 1 s)**
- Proteins diffuse from bulk to surface (transport-controlled regime).
- Adsorption is governed by a balance of:
  - Hydrophobic interactions
  - Electrostatic (Coulombic) interactions
  - van der Waals forces
  - Hydrogen bonding
  - Structural rearrangements in the protein

**Step 4 — Conformational Change / Rearrangement (seconds – hours)**
- Once adsorbed, proteins may **denature** (lose tertiary structure) to maximize favorable surface contacts.
- This exposes hidden (cryptic) amino acid sequences, e.g., the **RGD sequence** (Arg-Gly-Asp) in fibronectin.
- Denaturation can trigger adverse responses (e.g., fibrinogen unfolding exposing platelet-binding sites → thrombosis).

**Step 5 — Cell Adhesion & Biological Response (hours – days)**
- Cells (platelets, leukocytes, fibroblasts, osteoblasts) adhere via **integrin receptors** binding to specific peptide sequences of the adsorbed protein layer.
- This is the **"real" biological response** to the implant.

### 2.2 Why Does This Matter? — The "Why & How"

> **Why** do proteins adsorb so readily? Because adsorption reduces the system's total free energy, $\Delta G_{ads} < 0$. In an aqueous medium, the dominant contribution is the **hydrophobic effect**: when non-polar regions of the protein contact a hydrophobic surface, ordered water molecules around hydrophobic patches are released, increasing the system's entropy — an enthalpically driven process.

> **How** is selectivity decided? The **Vroman Effect** states that the protein with the highest mobility and surface affinity arrives first, then is progressively displaced by larger, more strongly binding proteins that arrive later. The dynamic protein layer is constantly evolving.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G_{ads} = \gamma_{SB} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LB}$ | Young's Dupré equation: free energy of adsorption | J/m² |
| 2 | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$ | Young's equation (wetting) | mN/m |
| 3 | $W_{a} = \gamma_{SG} + \gamma_{LG} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Work of adhesion (Dupré) | mN/m |
| 4 | $J = -D \, \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law of protein flux to surface | mol/(m²·s) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law (diffusion in solution) | — |
| 6 | $t_{diff} \approx \dfrac{x^2}{2D}$ | Approx. diffusion time to travel distance $x$ | s |
| 7 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{C_0 \, \sqrt{D t}}{\sqrt{\pi}}$ | Surface excess (for diffusion-limited adsorption) | mol/m² |
| 8 | $\theta = 0$ → $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{LG}$ | Complete wetting condition | — |
| 9 | $S = \gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LG}$ | Spreading coefficient | mN/m |
| 10 | $k_B T \approx 4.1 \times 10^{-21}$ J | Thermal energy at 300 K (sets the scale for adsorption) | J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
